notes.anchor: the very head of the bill
notes.note: Clodius's law of exile (the lex Clodia de capite civis) had a sanction clause forbidding any motion for Cicero's recall to be raised in the Senate; hence "there is silence."

notes.anchor: the same tribune of the plebs in office
notes.note: P. Clodius Pulcher, tribune of the plebs in 58 BC, whose bills exiled Cicero and who remained in office to block any recall.
A consul-elect for 57 BC hostile to Cicero --- most likely Q. Metellus Nepos, who entered office an enemy though he was later won over to support the recall.

notes.anchor: the speech that has come out
notes.note: An unpublished invective speech of Cicero's against an opponent (probably Clodius or Curio) that had leaked into circulation against his will. Cicero is alarmed by its appearance and wants it disowned.
Cicero asks Atticus to do damage control over the leaked speech, treating its escape as a fresh injury to him.

notes.anchor: it can be shown not to be mine
notes.note: Cicero hopes the leaked speech can be denied as a forgery, citing its carelessness and the fact that he never traded a word with the man it attacks.

notes.anchor: the sixteenth day before the Kalends of August
notes.note: Counting backward inclusively from 1 August (here called by its old name Sextilis), this is 17 July 58 BC.
